description: Optimizing the notification feed query on a 1.2B-row notifications table. Review the index design choice and whether existing indexes should be pruned.
review_checklist: Verify column ordering matches query equality + range + sort pattern
Identify INCLUDE opportunities for covering index
Distinguish DROP INDEX safety on hot tables

ground_truth.description: Index column ordering is wrong for the target query. PostgreSQL B-tree composite indexes require equality predicates BEFORE range/sort columns. The query filters on user_id (equality) and is_read (equality), then sorts by created_at. Correct ordering: `(user_id, is_read, created_at DESC)`. The proposed `(created_at DESC, user_id, is_read)` cannot use the index for the user_id equality filter — planner must scan all created_at entries within the range.

Missing INCLUDE clause for covering index. The query SELECTs id, title, created_at — these should be in the index payload via `INCLUDE (title, id)` to avoid heap fetches (index-only scan). For 1.2B rows and a 50-row query, this is the difference between 2ms (index-only) and 80-200ms (with heap fetches).

DROP INDEX (without CONCURRENTLY) on a hot table acquires AccessExclusiveLock. On a 1.2B-row table during peak hours, even the metadata-only DROP can block briefly under heavy concurrent load. Use DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

Pre-existing index `idx_notif_user_created (created_at, user_id)` is redundant and useless — same column-order mistake as the new index. It cannot serve queries with user_id equality filter. Should be dropped, not retained alongside the new index.

Pre-existing `idx_notif_user_read (user_id, is_read)` is partially redundant with the optimal new index (user_id, is_read, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (...). The two-column variant is dominated by the three-column variant for any query that filters user_id+is_read. Audit and drop as part of this migration.

Index idx_notif_user (user_id) is a strict prefix of (user_id, is_read, created_at DESC) and is therefore redundant. Drop it. Each redundant index doubles INSERT cost and consumes ~25GB of buffer cache for this table.
